EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep strong low pressure system moved down the west coast and through the Great Basin and south of the Four Corners region, providing very cold air and ample lift to result in significant snowfall for the mountains and valleys of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado. Snow bands also set up across southeast Utah allowing for some areas to see locally higher amounts. While the central Colorado mountains also saw some snow, totals were not nearly as much as the southern mountains. A much colder, arctic air mass was left in this system's wake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 14 inches of snow fell in the La Sal Mountains with 18 to 21 inches in the Abajo Mountains. The town of Monticello also received 16 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep strong low pressure system moved down the west coast and through the Great Basin and south of the Four Corners region, providing very cold air and ample lift to result in significant snowfall for the mountains and valleys of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado. Snow bands also set up across southeast Utah allowing for some areas to see locally higher amounts. While the central Colorado mountains also saw some snow, totals were not nearly as much as the southern mountains. A much colder, arctic air mass was left in this system's wake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area with banding resulting in 10.8 inches of snow at Natural Bridges National Monument. Gusts were in the 25 to 35 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong atmospheric river coming in from the west in conjunction with a low pressure system dropping southward out of the Pacific Northwest tracked along a strong northwest to southeast oriented jet. This led to significant snowfall across all mountain ranges of western Colorado with lesser amounts across the La Sals in southeast Utah. Some lower northwest and west-central higher valleys even saw moderate snowfall. Strong winds in the higher elevations continued to bring blowing and drifting snow as well as whiteout conditions and road closures to most mountain passes. A strong arctic front dropped in from the north late in the storms period, leading to some of the whiteout concerns and bringing continued snow in the Uncompahgre Gorge and north facing slopes. Snowfall was limited in the lower valleys due to cold arctic air being scoured out from the winds leading up to this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 9 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains. Gusts generally stayed in the 20s and 30s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief respite from a more modest winter system in the preceding days, another stronger winter system moved through the Intermountain West. This system brought accumulating snow to much of Utah, with some areas receiving more substantial amounts. Additionally, a fatal vehicle accident occurred in this time as a result of black ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of COOP observers, CoCoRaHS observers, and public reports included snowfall amounts of approximately 6 to 12 inches of snow. Some selected snowfall reports include 6.0 inches at 1 E Ogden, 9.0 inches at Fruit Heights, and 12.0 inches at 1 NE Pleasant View. The majority of this snow fell from the evening of 1/6 through the morning of 1/7, with lighter lingering snow persisting around the area through early 1/8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved from northwest to southeast across all of eastern Utah and western Colorado, with significant banding and a long lived snow squall that accompanied the cold front. This resulted in strong winds with gusts 45 to 55 mph in the valleys along the boundary with gusts 60 to 80 mph in the mountains.  Low visibilities and whiteout conditions also occurred at times along this frontal boundary due to falling and blowing snow.  Accumulations in the lower elevations were 2 inches or less in spots due to the brief nature of the snowfall along the front as well as milder temperatures, but the impacts due to the snow squalls were felt across much of the region as temperatures plummeted behind it. The San Juan mountains received the heaviest snow with the central Colorado mountains receiving moderate snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted in the 30 to 45 mph range with a peak wind gust of 46 mph. Webcams indicated low visibility in snow squalls among the higher elevations of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier was injured when he was caught in two successive avalanches. A group of skiers above him, witnessed the avalanches when triggered, and called for assistance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time is unknown. A group of three skiers noticed a solo skier was caught in an avalanche in Little Cottonwood Canyon, then carried 1,500 feet by a second avalanche. A member of the group skied down to the caught individual, and called for assistance, with the injured skier then being lifted from the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought strong winds, then mountain snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time is unknown. Four skiers were caught in an avalanche near Big Cottonwood Canyon. One of the skiers had shoulder and upper leg pain and was not able to ski out. The other skiers called for help, and the injured skier was lifted out. The others skied out after medics recovered the injured skier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure trough moved into the Pacific Northwest and dropped south through the Great Basin in the days leading up to March 1st. At the base of this trough, a 160 knot jet aloft tracked overhead of eastern Utah and western Colorado beginning on the 1st, with daytime heating starting to mix down these much stronger southwest winds. Winds peaked during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 2nd as the pressure gradient through the atmosphere tightened up ahead of a strong cold front. Strongest winds were observed just prior to the cold front's passage, and led to numerous reports of power outages, downed trees, and even a few small brush fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust of 62 mph was recoded at the Vernal mesonet station EW3712 at 2:55 PM on the 2nd. Other gusts around the zone ranged from 45 mph to 60 mph over the course of the day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 59 mph was recorded at the mesonet site PC188, located at Hole N The Rock, at 4:20 PM on the 2nd. Other gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 55 mph during this period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust of 77 mph occurred at the UDOT mesonet site UTHAD, located on I-70 at MP 226 Harley Dome, at 5:30 PM on the 2nd. The KCNY ASOS recorded two gusts between 60 mph and 65 mph between 4:00 PM and 5:00 PM. Also during this time period, two other mesonet sites in the zone recorded gusts of 65-70 mph. Gusts ranged from 45-80 mph during the duration of the event across Utah Zone 027.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late on the 13th and into the day on the 14th, a large closed low pressure system developed over the southern Great Basin. In response to this feature, the pressure gradient over northeastern Utah and northwestern Colorado began to tighten up substantially. Strong, gusty winds materialized during the day on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at the EW3712 mesonet site in Vernal, CO at 11:00 AM on the 14th. This site also recorded an above criteria gust of 66 mph at 1:35 PM on the same day. Across the zone, gusts ranged from 45 mph to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 170 knot jet associated with an upper level trough moved into the Great Basin late on the 22nd and into the 23rd. While the strongest of the southwest winds remained well south of the Grand Junction area of responsibility, the combination of these stronger winds mixing down and some localized downsloping off the terrain resulted in areas of high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust of 59 mph was recorded at the UDOT mesonet station UTHAD, located on I-70 at MP 226 Harley Dome. Gusts across the zone ranged from 50 mph to 60 mph during the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front and upper level trough moved through the area from northwest to southeast, bringing moderate snow to the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The La Sal Mountains in Southeast Utah and the Northwest San Juan Mountains were favored for the heaviest snowfall accumulations due to favored northerly orographics and colder air in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 8 inches of snow at Mosby Mountain. Winds gusted in the 30 to 40 mph range on average with a peak gust of 49 at Little Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 18 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains with around 2 inches across the Abajo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low moved through Eastern Utah and settled south of the Four Corners region where it stalled and became cut off. This resulted in abundant Pacific moisture circulating around the low pressure system and very heavy snowfall for the Eastern Utah and Southwest Colorado Mountains. The Abajo Mountains of Southeast Utah and San Juan Mountains of Southwest Colorado received the most snowfall due to the stalled nature of the low where 2 to 3 feet of snow fell. Warmer temperatures also resulted in higher snow levels and limited snow accumulation in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 15 inches at Hickerson Park, which is favored in northeast flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a shortwave trough moved through the area, bringing light to moderate snow accumulations to the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado. Northern slopes appeared favored for the better snow accumulations with limited amounts in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 10 inches at Hickerson Park, which is favored in northeast flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred ahead of a strong colf front that resulted in a high wind event and snow levels dropping to all valley floors after frontal passage. Signficant moisture supplied by an Atmospheric River fueled this storm system as it dropped in from the Intermountain West and brought feet of snow across the northern and central mountains of northeast Utah and western Colorado, with much less across southwest Colorado. Some lower northern and central valleys of western Colorado also saw significant snow with near white out conditions and banded snowfall or squall conditions at times. Winds continued to be strong throughout this event with gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range in the valleys and 60 to 80 mph range in the mountains on average.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 60 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly long duration two part winter storm moved through Utah in mid January. This winter storm resulted in several days of fairly substantial mountain snow, with more modest accumulations noted in some of the lower elevation valleys. Mountain snow was more or less a continuous feature with minimal breaks between the two portions of the system, while most valley accumulations occurred during the colder second half of the system. This system also produced periods of strong sustained winds and gusts, and was responsible for triggering multiple avalanches throughout Utah's mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 6:20 am MST, a natural avalanche occurred near the Dugway section of Logan Canyon. Per the Utah Avalanche Center report, this slab avalanche of wind-drifted snow started around 6000 feet on a west facing slope, had a vertical run of 150 feet, a width of 80 feet, and a depth of 2 feet. The avalanche hit a car on US-89 and pushed it against a guard rail, but no injuries were reported. This avalanche resulted in closure of US-89 in Logan Canyon between milepost 460 (near Logan) and milepost 490 (near Garden City/Bear Lake) for over 24 hours.

Additional reports included 34.0 inches at Heber City 10.1 ESE CoCoRaHS, and a public report of 16.0 inches at 6 E Heber City. Strong winds were also noted, with several observations at the Windy Peak mesonet site in excess of 70 mph (with a peak gust of 98 mph at 5:00 pm MST 1/12), and wind gusts at or above 70 mph at the Trial Lake mesonet site.

Additionally, strong wind gusts were observed through the event, including a gust of 59 mph at Trappers Loop Road mesonet site, a gust of 65 mph at the Eden mesonet site, a 61 mph gust at the I-80 @ Silver Ck Jct mesonet site, and several gusts in excess of 60 mph (peak gust of 69 mph at 6:50 pm MST 1/12) at the US-189 at Deer Creek Dam mesonet site.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of several skiers were backcountry skiing in the of Porter Fork area within Millcreek Canyon on Friday, January 12. The group completed multiple runs in the West Porter Fork portion, and on their third ascent, opted to exit Main Porter Fork from West Porter Fork. As they were exiting, an avalanche was triggered, and one of the skiers in the group was unable to get to a safe location before the slide caught and fully buried him. After several minutes of beacon searching, they were able to successfully locate and probe the buried skier approximately 4 feet deep. The group worked quickly to uncover the buried skier, and once uncovered, it was discovered the buried skier was unconscious and was breathing very shallowly. While calling emergency services and deliberating who should preform CPR on the injured skier, the skier gradually began to show improved breathing, and slowly regained consciousness. While further injuries were initially feared, after some time the skier was able to ski out of the area. The Utah Avalanche Center danger rating for the day was listed as High at elevations above 8000 feet, and Considerable at elevations below 8000 feet. Specific timing of the avalanche was not stated, thus event time is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of snowmobilers (estimated at 5 total) were riding in American Fork Canyon Saturday January 13 at an elevation of approximately 10000 feet on a northeast aspect. An avalanche was unintentionally triggered, catching 3 of the members, resulting in partial burial of 2 members, and full burial of 1 member. Of the 2 partial burials, one member deployed an airbag and was able to dig themselves out. The other was buried with a hand sticking out, and was able to be immediately dug out by other group members (with no injuries reported). The group member who was fully buried was buried for around 5 minutes before the group could locate and uncover them. They were unconscious and reportedly turning blue, but regained consciousness thereafter with no further injury reported. An avalanche accident and rescue summary was  reported by a third party, and specific timing of the avalanche was not stated, thus the event time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief respite from a more modest winter system in the preceding days, another stronger winter system moved through the Intermountain West. This system brought accumulating snow to much of Utah, with some areas receiving more substantial amounts. Additionally, a fatal vehicle accident occurred in this time as a result of black ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1:20 am MST on 1/6, an incident was reported to law enforcement of an accident involving an F-150 pickup truck travelling on southbound I-15 near milepost 81, just north of Paragonah. Utah State Highway Patrol concluded that the driver lost control of the vehicle on black ice, causing the vehicle to spin left into the median, roll over, and eject all three occupants of the vehicle before coming to rest on its side blocking the northbound lanes of the interstate. Of the three occupants, an adult female (age 26) died at the scene, an injured adult male (age not specified) was transported to the hospital via ground transportation, and an injured female infant (age 1) was transported to the hospital via helicopter transportation. The occupants were reported to not be wearing seatbelts, which in turn was deemed a likely contribution to the severity of the crash. Despite both the adult male and infant female being described as in critical condition for a period following the accident, both are reported to have recovered. The northbound lanes of I-15 near the crash site were closed for approximately 2.5 hours following the incident in order to allow Utah Highway Patrol to conduct the investigation.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed and moved southward through northern Utah during the evening hours on both January 9 and January 10, bringing impacts to the evening commutes. Utah Highway Patrol reportedly responded to 101 vehicle accidents on 1/9, and 143 vehicle accidents on 1/10. A portion of these will be included in rough event damage estimates. Surprisingly, no injuries were reported with these accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Multiple mesonet stations recorded periods of strong wind gusts (multiple periods in excess of 30 mph) and significant reductions to visibility as a well defined band of snow moved into the area, and gradually began to fall apart as the evening progressed. Some examples include the I-80 @ Grassey mesonet site (peak wind gust of 43 mph, visibility below 1/2 of a mile), the I-80 at MP 68 West Delle mesonet site (peak wind gust of 43 mph, visibility below 1/2 of a mile), and the I-80 @ mp 1 mesonet site (peak wind gust of 43 mph, visibility of 1/2 of a mile). Many of these mesonet sites also reported temperatures rapidly falling below freezing as this snow squall passed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. At 4:45 pm MST, the Clarkston mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 54 mph as a well defined band of snow moved through. Between 5:00 pm MST and 5:30 pm MST, the Logan-Cache Airport ASOS recorded a period of wind gusts in excess of 30 mph with a peak gust of 52 mph as this feature moved through. During this time, the ASOS also recorded periods of heavy snow and visibility to as low as 1/4 of a mile. This snow squall continued southward through the area thereafter. Many mesonet sites also reported temperatures rapidly falling below freezing as this snow squall passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed and moved southward through northern Utah during the evening hours on both January 9 and January 10, bringing impacts to the evening commutes. Utah Highway Patrol reportedly responded to 101 vehicle accidents on 1/9, and 143 vehicle accidents on 1/10. A portion of these will be included in rough event damage estimates. Surprisingly, no injuries were reported with these accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Multiple mesonet stations recorded periods of strong wind gusts (multiple periods in excess of 30 mph) and significant reductions to visibility as a well defined band of snow moved into the area, and gradually began to fall apart as the evening progressed. Some examples include the I-80 @ mp 78 mesonet site (peak wind gust of 59 mph, visibility below 1/2 of a mile), the I-80 at MP 79 Timpie mesonet site (peak wind gust of 65 mph), the I-80 at MP 81 Dolomite mesonet site (peak wind gust of 56 mph), and the Grantsville South mesonet site (peak wind gust of 54 mph). Additionally the Bolinder Field-Tooele Valley Airport AWOS reported a max wind gust of 37 mph with visibility to 1 3/4 of a mile as the band started to become less defined. Many of these mesonet sites also reported temperatures rapidly falling below freezing as this snow squall passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly long duration two part winter storm moved through Utah in mid January. This winter storm resulted in several days of fairly substantial mountain snow, with more modest accumulations noted in some of the lower elevation valleys. Mountain snow was more or less a continuous feature with minimal breaks between the two portions of the system, while most valley accumulations occurred during the colder second half of the system. This system also produced periods of strong sustained winds and gusts, and was responsible for triggering multiple avalanches throughout Utah's mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of COOP and CoCoRaHS observer reports included snowfall amounts of 11.3 inches at Hyrum 0.8 SE, 10.9 inches at Logan Radio KVNU, 10.3 inches at Trenton, 7.0 inches at Smithfield, and 7.0 inches at Logan 0.9 NNE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the northern portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado and stalled along I-70, resulting in moderate snowfall and strong winds across the Eastern Uinta Mountains of Northeast Utah, as well as all mountain ranges of Northwest Colorado and West-Central Colorado. A low pressure trough also moved through the area and interacted with the cold front, resulting in locally higher amounts due to banding along the front, even into some higher northwest Colorado valleys. Milder temperatures resulted in very little snow in the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range on March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave and closed mid level low moved across the southern half of eastern Utah and western Colorado following the last strong Atmospheric River system, with colder air already in place from the previous storm. This led to the valleys of southwest Colorado and southeast Utah also seeing some significant snowfall with the mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado favored for the better accumulations of 5 to 12 inches on average. The valleys and mountains of northeast Utah and far northwest Colorado also saw some moderate snowfall given the cold air and forcing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 87 mph was recorded at the Central Wasatch Peaks - West mesonet site. Snowfall accumulation was generally below warning criteria, but Little Cottonwood Canyon was closed from around 11:30 p.m. on February 26 through 8:30 a.m. on February 27 for avalanche mitigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southwesterly jet aloft ahead of a deep Pacific low pressure system moved over eastern Utah and western Colorado starting early on the 26th. As the day progressed, these strong winds began to mix down, bringing strong gusts of 40-50 mph across much of the area. These winds maximized ahead of a potent cold front that begin impacting northern portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado late on the 26th, and finally moved south of the Four Corners around midday on the 27th. During this period, gusts above 58 mph in the valleys and above 75 mph in the mountains were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranged from 45-65 mph across the lower elevations of Utah Zone 024 between roughly noon on the 26th and mid-morning on the 27th. The peak recorded gust was 64 mph at 3:30PM, and was recorded at the mesonet station EW3712 Vernal, roughly 2 miles southwest of the town of Maeser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Atmospheric River and strong southerly flow moved into the Four Corners region, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow to the southern mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado, expanding into the central Colorado mountains. Snow continued in colder northwest flow behind the trough, bringing additional moderate to heavy snow to the northwest Colorado mountains and higher valleys. Milder temps limited accumulations in lower elevations with all mountain areas seeing significant snowfall. Highest wind gusts remained above 12,000 feet with gusts 45 to 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Wind gusts were generally 15 to 35 mph with 45 to 50 mph gusts observed at West Stateline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture due to a significant Atmospheric River moved through the Four Corners into eastern Utah and southwest Colorado, favoring heavy snowfall for the southern slopes across the eastern Utah mountains and mountains of southwest and west-central Colorado as well as very strong winds above 11,000 feet. Warm air ahead of this system limited snow accumulations for the lower elevations. The northern and central mountains also received moderate snow in the colder northwest flow portion of the event but not as much snow as the southern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 24 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds generally gusted 35 to 45 mph with peak gusts of 50 to 65 mph on February 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring season shortwave trough moved through, resulting in isolated to scattered convection. Isolated strong wind gusts were observed as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milford Municipal Airport/Ben and Judy Briscoe Field ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 75 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of dry and mild ridge dominated weather, a shortwave flattened the ridge and shifted the axis east, resulting in a period of more unsettled weather followed by a mostly dry cold frontal passage. Convective showers and thunderstorms during this time resulted in isolated strong wind gusts, and decaying convection resulted in a localized heat burst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Provo Municipal Airport AWOS recorded a wind gust of 58 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Speirs Fire started in mid July due to a lightning strike 3 miles south of Dutch John, Utah which is south of the Flaming Gorge Reservoir.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Speirs Fire started in mid July due to a lightning strike 3 miles south of Dutch John, Utah which is south of the Flaming Gorge Reservoir. The fire grew to 715 acres before it was contained in mid to late August. The estimated cost was $9,609,000 to date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough moisture and instability lingered across the higher terrain of eastern Utah and western Colorado to form showers and thunderstorms. The most intense convection was observed across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where a passing weak shortwave and a modest upper level jet provided additional lift and instability. The probability of strong winds was also enhanced by dry surface levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed over the higher terrain of northeastern Utah on the afternoon of August 7. As these storms developed, there were enhanced by some upper level support in the form of a passing wave and an upper level jet. This allowed the storms to grow strong enough to potentially produce severe threats, such as wind in excess of 58 mph and hail in excess of 1 inch. Additionally, dry surface layers would enhance already potentially strong outflow winds. All this combined to produce a 90 mph gust at the Winter Ridge RAWS, located in the Utah Tavaputs Plateau. No reports of damage or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of dry and mild ridge dominated weather, a shortwave flattened the ridge and shifted the axis east, resulting in a period of more unsettled weather followed by a mostly dry cold frontal passage. Convective showers and thunderstorms during this time resulted in isolated strong wind gusts, and decaying convection resulted in a localized heat burst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Starting at 12:00 AM MST, a decaying convective cell collapsed over the Ogden-Hinckley Airport resulting in a heat burst. This heat burst was characterized by a period of severe wind gusts, with the Ogden-Hinckley Airport ASOS recording a period of gusts to 78 mph, followed by a final gust to 64 mph before winds subsided. Temperatures initially around 75F or so prior to winds increasing peaked at 83F during the strongest winds. Corresponding to this time range, dew points initially around 40F dropped to a minimum of 28F. These strong winds were seemingly localized to the airport, but no damage was ultimately reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough moisture and instability lingered across the higher terrain of eastern Utah and western Colorado to form showers and thunderstorms. The most intense convection was observed across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where a passing weak shortwave and a modest upper level jet provided additional lift and instability. The probability of strong winds was also enhanced by dry surface levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With just enough moisture to work with, showers and thunderstorms began to form on the higher terrain around the Uinta Basin early in the afternoon of August 7. These storms received some upper level support in the form of a passing shortwave and a modest upper level jet, which allowed them to become strong to severe. In addition, dry surface levels acted to boost already strong to severe winds. Winds recorded during this event ranged from 45 mph to the peak of 64 mph, which was recorded at a Utah State Climate Mesonet Site 9.1 miles east of Randlett. No reports of damage or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Ridge Fire started in late August 40 miles north-northeast of Castle Valley in the Westwater Canyon Wilderness Area near the Utah-Colorado state line just south of Interstate 70 due to a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Black Ridge Fire started in late August 40 miles north-northeast of Castle Valley in the Westwater Canyon Wilderness Area near the Utah-Colorado state line just south of Interstate 70 due to a lightning strike. The fire grew to 305 acres before it was contained a couple days later in late August. The estimated cost to date was $75,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak cold front and low pressure trough that tracked across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. Given the dry air at the lower levels, thunderstorms were high based with gusty outflow winds being the primary threat from storms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 58 mph across Grand County in east-central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 66 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Regional Airport near Moab, Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 70 to 80 knot jet streak moved over eastern Utah with deep layer shear and CAPE between 1000 to 1500 J/kg, resulting in a few strong storms that produced hail dime to penny size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong storm passed over Castle Valley area and produced hail dime to penny size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over Texas and low pressure over the Baja Coast kept a deep southerly flow of monsoonal moisture across the Four Corners region. Training storms led to significant flash flooding across southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from a thunderstorm fell upstream of Indian Creek and Salt Creek Wash between 1630 and 1800. Water flowed through both Indian Creek and Salt Creek Wash where it intersects with Highway 211 in Canyonlands National Park Needles District causing flash flooding with water flowing rapidly through the washes with some small tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A distinct moisture gradient existed ahead of and behind a cold front situated across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. Scattered showers and storms developed ahead of this boundary and given dry air at low levels, storms were high based producing virga and strong outflow winds. Wind gusts exceeded 58 mph in Grand county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Regional Airport near Moab.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold season type of trough and associated surface features moved through Utah in early May. Ahead of the system, strong synoptic wind gusts occurred throughout portions of the state. While wind gusts decreased following a cold frontal passage, precipitation increased. Periods of post-frontal precipitation then continued in the unsettled northwesterly flow as the trough shifted through the region. Given anomalously cold air associated with the trough, some area mountains received Winter Storm levels of snow, and sub-warning snow amounts were even observed in some lower elevation valley locations. Following the systems departure, a fatal avalanche occurred involving multiple backcountry skiers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CAMEL BACK MTN mesonet site measured the first wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with an initial high wind gust of 62 mph observed 5/4 at 7:25 pm MST. The Lakeside Mountain mesonet site measured the last wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with a final high wind gust of 60 mph observed 5/5 at 8:40 am MST. The peak wind gust during the event was a gust of 72 mph observed at the English Village mesonet site 5/4 at 10:40 pm MST and also recorded 5/5 at 12:05 am MST by the CAMEL BACK MTN mesonet site. Finally, some additional selected observed gusts at mesonet sites included 70 mph at I-80 at Grassey, 68 mph at Sr-196 At Mp 2 Dugway, and 59 mph at I-80 At Mp 68 West Delle. Many sites recorded multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph throughout the event.

Following the systems departure, a fatal avalanche occurred involving multiple backcountry skiers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The I-80 at MP 81 Dolomite mesonet site measured the first wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with an initial high wind gust of 58 mph observed 5/4 at 9:20 pm MST. The VERNON HILL mesonet site measured the last wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with a final high wind gust of 67 mph observed 5/5 at 8:00 am MST. The SR-201 at I-80 mesonet site measured the zone's peak wind gust during the event, with a gust of 85 mph observed 5/4 at 11:40 pm MST. Finally, some additional selected observed gusts at mesonet sites in the zone through the event included 72 mph at both SR-36 at MP 49 Stockton Bar and I-80 at MP 81 Dolomite, 62 mph at Grantsville South, and 58 mph at I-80 At MP 79 Timpie. Many sites recorded multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold season type of trough and associated surface features moved through Utah in early May. Ahead of the system, strong synoptic wind gusts occurred throughout portions of the state. While wind gusts decreased following a cold frontal passage, precipitation increased. Periods of post-frontal precipitation then continued in the unsettled northwesterly flow as the trough shifted through the region. Given anomalously cold air associated with the trough, some area mountains received Winter Storm levels of snow, and sub-warning snow amounts were even observed in some lower elevation valley locations. Following the systems departure, a fatal avalanche occurred involving multiple backcountry skiers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Five Points Lake, Lily Lake, and Lakefork Basin recorded 1.2 to 1.9 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 20 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media disseminated a picture of a large tree toppled and on a car near the Cotton Bottom Restaurant near the mouth of Big Cottonwood Canyon. Highest winds and gusts throughout the Salt Lake Valley occurred approximately between 1:00 pm MST and 4:00 pm MST, with many observations of gusts in the 30 mph to 45 mph range. Given very wet soils and trees beginning to gain foliage, the damage was associated with an estimated wind gust of 45 mph, though locally higher gusts may have been possible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold season type of trough and associated surface features moved through Utah in early May. Ahead of the system, strong synoptic wind gusts occurred throughout portions of the state. While wind gusts decreased following a cold frontal passage, precipitation increased. Periods of post-frontal precipitation then continued in the unsettled northwesterly flow as the trough shifted through the region. Given anomalously cold air associated with the trough, some area mountains received Winter Storm levels of snow, and sub-warning snow amounts were even observed in some lower elevation valley locations. Following the systems departure, a fatal avalanche occurred involving multiple backcountry skiers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of three backcountry skiers set out early in the morning to ski in the Big Willow Aprons area near Lone Peak. After skiing for several hours without issue, an unintentionally triggered soft slab avalanche occurred on the north aspect of the slope (approximately 40 degree angle) around 10,600 feet elevation. The avalanche was approximately 18 inches deep, 150 feet wide, and had a vertical run of 800 feet. All three skiers were caught by the avalanche. One skier was partially buried and was able to self-extricate. After self-extricating, the skier attempted to find the transceiver signal of the buried skiers. Approximately 13 minutes after the avalanche, the skier successfully probed one of the buried skiers and uncovered them, but they were ultimately unresponsive when rescue breaths were attempted. In the subsequent minutes, the second buried skier was probed and uncovered, but also found to be unresponsive to any resuscitation attempts. Around 1-1.5 hours after the avalanche, a helicopter ambulance was able to hoist the injured skier out, and this skier was subsequently taken to the hospital and released later in the day. Due to unfavorable weather, search and rescue recovered the deceased skiers bodies the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A section of a shortwave trough broke off from the greater northern stream flow and retrograded southwestward through Utah, eventually becoming positioned in a manner to allow easterly downslope winds to initiate across prone areas of Utah's northern Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US-89 at Park Lane mesonet site measured the first wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with an initial high wind gust of 61 mph observed 5/9 at 10:00 pm MST. The US-89 at Park Lane mesonet site also measured the last wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with a final high wind gust of 64 mph observed 5/10 at 8:10 am MST. The US-89 at Park Lane mesonet site also measured the zone's peak wind gust during the event, with a gust of 66 mph observed 5/10 at 1:15 am MST. Between 5:15 am MST and 6:15 am MST 5/10, the US-89 at Park Lane mesonet site also measured a period of sustained winds at or above 40 mph. Finally, some additional selected observed gusts at mesonet sites in the zone through the event time included 64 mph at CENEWS, 64 mph at KJ7NO-2 Farmington, 60 mph at Centerville, and 59 mph at US-89 at MP 406 Weber River. Many sites recorded multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph throughout the event. Given this was a downslope-style wind event, the highest concentration of sustained winds and gusts were closer to the terrain rather than spread further across the zone.

The Milford Municipal Airport/Ben and Judy Briscoe Field ASOS measured the last wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with a final high wind gust of 59 mph observed 5/5 at 10:50 am MST. The I-15 at MP 57 Cedar City mesonet site measured the zone's peak wind gust during the event, with a gust of 71 mph observed 5/5 at 4:20 am MST. Additionally, the Cedar City Regional Airport ASOS measured a period of 59 mph wind gusts between 8:35 am MST and 8:55 am MST 5/5. Many of these sites recorded multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred ahead of a strong cold front that brought much colder air as well as moderate to heavy snow across the northern and central mountains of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. The Elkhead and Park Range Mountains as well as the Flattop Mountains in northwest Colorado saw the heaviest snowfall amounts with snowfall accumulations over a foot and locally higher up to 2 feet. Moderate snow also occurred across the mountains of southeast Utah with lesser amounts across the San Juan mountains. Strong winds continued to accompany the snowfall due to the change in air mass resulting in blowing snow and low visibility at times, but road impacts were limited to the higher passes due to wet roads in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted in the 45 to 55 mph range with a peak wind gust of 61 mph at UT43W Stateline.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 12 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 4 to 7 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 65 mph. The peak recorded gust was 62 mph on the evening of the 6th, recorded at a Citizen Weather Observation Program Mesonet site in Vernal. While this was the only recorded gust above 58 mph, gusts above 45 mph were recorded through the duration of the event, even into the post-cold frontal regime on the evening of the 7th. Peak gusts on the evening of the 6th were associated with the immediately pre-frontal airmass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 70 mph during this event. The peak gusts of 67 mph were both recorded on the evening of the 7th at KCNY ASOS at the Canyonlands Airport north of Moab. These peak gusts actually occurred in the post-frontal regime, but gusts of 50 mph were common from the evening of the 5th on through the cold frontal passage on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an unseasonably strong Pacific trough moved inland in the days leading up to this wind event, the pressure gradient ahead of it began to tighten. An accompanying deep layer jet streak brought an additional boost to winds ahead of this trough. Although the winds began early on the morning of the 5th, they lasted through the cold front passage on the 7th, particularly at higher elevations. Multiple wind highlights, with a combination of High Wind Warnings and Wind Advisories, were in effect for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 60 mph during this event. The peak gust of 60 mph was recorded at the Hole N The Rock Mesonet station located about 12 miles south of Moab on the afternoon of the 5th. 3 gusts of 58 mph or greater were recorded at this site on the 5th. The majority of peak gusts occurred on the 5th, with a secondary peak on the afternoon of the 7th associated with the cold front passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening trough over the Pacific Northwest phased with a potent shortwave over the Baja Peninsula, pulling a strong jet northeastward toward the Four Corners. This, combined with generally clear skies that promoted deep mixing, allowed strong winds to reach the surface on the afternoon of the 20th. This event was confined mainly to Southeast Utah and Southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 60 mph. The peak wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the KCNY ASOS at the Canyonlands Airport, north of Moab. This was the only wind gust to be recorded above 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled out just north of the Colorado/Wyoming border late on the 16th and into the early hours of the 17th. This front remained in place through the evening of the 18th before an approaching wave finally pushed it out of the way. Ahead of this boundary, the pressure gradient tightened up enough to allow for some strong wind gusts across portions of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the cold front became more mobile on the 19th, portions of northeastern Utah experienced some high winds. Gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 60 mph. The peak gusts of 61 mph were recorded at the Utah Department of Transportation Mesonet site US-40 At Mp 140 Asphalt Ridge and at the Vernal West Mesonet station. Both of these gusts occurred in the early afternoon on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another passing cold front was kicked south into central portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado on the 23rd. In the post-frontal regime, strong northwesterly winds and some downsloping brought high winds to portions of northeastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 65 mph during this event. The peak recorded wind gust was 64 mph at the Vernal Citizen Weather Observation Program mesonet location in Vernal, on the evening of the 23rd. Peak wind gusts occurred after the cold front passed and winds switched to northwesterly, with favors downsloping in that part of the Uinta Basin and enhances wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As yet another strong Pacific trough approached eastern Utah and western Colorado with a cold front in tow, strong and gusty southwest winds broke out across the region. The majority of these gusts remained below highlight criteria, but a few locations gusts above thresholds did occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to near 65 mph. The peak recorded gust was 66 mph at the KCNY ASOS at the Canyonlands Airport north of Moab. Peak gusts appear to have occurred in the post-frontal northwest flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact shortwave trough grazing the northern portions of the state resulted in a few isolated strong storms given a sufficiently unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SIMPSON SPRINGS mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 58 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front moved through the region, resulting in isolated reports of strong and damaging wind gusts. Additionally, reports and pictures were received of a funnel cloud in central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Videos and pictures were received via social media of a moderately sized rotating funnel cloud extending approximately halfway down from cloud base to ground near Delta. Location estimated based upon video and associated description.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of dry and mild ridge dominated weather, a shortwave flattened the ridge and shifted the axis east, resulting in a period of more unsettled weather followed by a mostly dry cold frontal passage. Convective showers and thunderstorms during this time resulted in isolated strong wind gusts, and decaying convection resulted in a localized heat burst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The I-215 at I-80 mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 58 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong mid level ridge slowly moved through the region, resulting in multiple days of excessive heat. At least one instance of injury and fatality were reported from the heat. The strong ridge eventually shifted far enough east and south of the area to allow sufficient moisture to return, resulting in the development of diurnal convection, with some thunderstorms yielding strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake Cty International Airport Center Field Observation mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 59 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern largely remained ridge-dominant continuing on through mid July, but sufficient moisture helped trigger isolated diurnal convection which produced a small number of strong wind gusts across Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The King Canyon mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 67 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

A following peak wind gust of 60 mph was subsequently recorded at 6:40 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A largely ridge-dominated pattern persisted, resulting in anomalously warm temperatures. These hot temperatures resulted in at least one instance of a heat related fatality. Despite the strong ridge, enough moisture continued to work into the area and help in the development of diurnal convection, some of which resulted in flash flooding over more rain sensitive areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56-year-old woman was hiking on a trail near Quail Creek State Park and reportedly ran out of water. When found by first responders the woman was unresponsive, and despite attempting life-saving measures, she ultimately did not survive. Temperatures nearby look to have peaked between around 103F to 105F during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A largely ridge-dominated pattern persisted, resulting in anomalously warm temperatures. These hot temperatures resulted in at least one instance of a heat related fatality. Despite the strong ridge, enough moisture continued to work into the area and help in the development of diurnal convection, some of which resulted in flash flooding over more rain sensitive areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A BLM Ranger reported a large flash flood of Dry Valley Creek where it meets Rock Springs Creek. The flash flood waters were reported to by up to 4-6 feet deep given a water mark left behind that was higher than the vehicle. Further south, a BLM Ranger reported flash flooding near the Old Town Paria site, with a couple feet of water reportedly flowing off the surrounding terrain and into the Paria River. Times estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paria River near Kanab river gage showed a rapid rise from minimal flow to a peak flow of 409 CFS. Flooding was also confirmed by an observer along the Paria River with supporting pictures and documentation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient moisture in combination with a shortwave impulse brushing by the area resulted in numerous strong thunderstorm wind gusts in addition to isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the evening, two swimmers at Grantsville Reservoir were pushed away from the shore as a result of high winds (from outflow associated with convection moving into the area). At approximately 8:30 pm MST a 9-1-1 call was placed stating that a swimmer had been pushed away from the shore. While one of the swimmers managed to return to shore, the other did not surface. Recovery efforts were initially unsuccessful, but the body of the swimmer that did not return to shore was later discovered two days later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Junction South mesonet station recorded initial peak wind gusts of 66 mph at 5:40 pm MST and 5:50 pm MST, and an additional strong gust of 65 mph at 6:00 pm MST. These winds were the result of thunderstorm outflow, with the thunderstorm also producing 0.27 inches of rain in 10 minutes at the mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong mid level ridge slowly moved through the region, resulting in multiple days of excessive heat. At least one instance of injury and fatality were reported from the heat. The strong ridge eventually shifted far enough east and south of the area to allow sufficient moisture to return, resulting in the development of diurnal convection, with some thunderstorms yielding strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple pictures and reports indicated wind damage in the Sugarhouse area associated with an area of decaying convection moving through. Reports included a healthy hardwood tree around 2 feet in diameter snapped at the trunk, with multiple 8 to 10 inch limbs down across the neighborhood. Additionally, reports mentioned many cars potentially totaled or significantly damaged as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure tracking east over California nudged the subtropical high eastward into the Southern Plains in the days leading up to this event. This allowed some monsoonal moisture to trickle into the Four Corners region. On the day of the event, the low crossed the Four Corners, adding some extra lift and instability. The most widespread coverage was south of the I-70 corridor, although that didn't stop a few showers and storms from firing off the higher terrain along and north of I-70. Dry surface levels kept the main threats to being gusty winds and maybe some small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whenever enough moisture and instability is present, a thunderstorm forms along the Utah Tavaputs near the McCook Ridge RAWS site. On this day, this RAWS site captured a wind gust to 81 mph at 1:27 MDT from the nearby thunderstorm. This storm also likely produced some small hail, although the remoteness of the location makes verification of that exceptionally difficult.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Speirs Fire started in mid July due to a lightning strike 3 miles south of Dutch John, Utah which is south of the Flaming Gorge Reservoir. The fire grew to 715 acres before it was contained in mid to late August. The estimated cost was $9,609,000 to date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong mid level ridge slowly moved through the region, resulting in multiple days of excessive heat. At least one instance of injury and fatality were reported from the heat. The strong ridge eventually shifted far enough east and south of the area to allow sufficient moisture to return, resulting in the development of diurnal convection, with some thunderstorms yielding strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brigham City Regional Airport AWOS recorded a wind gust of 61 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 2:30 pm MST, park rangers and search and rescue units from local law enforcement found and began treating two hikers in Snow Canyon State Park who were suffering from heat exhaustion. While treating the injured hikers, the units were notified by a passerby of an unconscious woman nearby. First responders subsequently located the unconscious woman, ultimately determining her to be deceased. Temperatures around the park at the time ranged from 107F at a CWOP site in Ivins to 99F at a higher elevation site near Yellow Knoll. The St George Regional Airport AWOS  was reporting temperatures of around 104F at the time. Excessive Heat Warnings were in effect for several days leading up to and during the event, and Snow Canyon State Park had been running a Drink Up campaign with signs at trailheads urging recreators to stay hydrated and limit outdoor activity during the hottest times of day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite high pressure building into the region, enough residual moisture from a monsoonal push earlier in the week remained to spawn afternoon convection over eastern Utah and western Colorado. Multiple weak waves added some extra lift and instability, while drying surface levels helped to enhance outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed near Green River, UT on the afternoon of the 25th and produced strong to severe wind gusts. Only one severe wind gust was recorded. This was 58 mph at the Utah Climate Center - AGMET mesonet site approximately 5 miles north of Green River. No reports of damages or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Babylon Fire started in early July due to a lightning strike 20 miles northwest of Blanding, Utah in the Manti-La Sal National Forest of the Abajo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Babylon Fire started in early July due to a lightning strike 20 miles northwest of Blanding, Utah in the Manti-La Sal National Forest of the Abajo Mountains. The fire grew to 199 acres before it was contained in mid July. The estimated final cost was $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of drier air was dropping into eastern Utah and western Colorado from the north on the afternoon of the 20th. As dry air filtered into the northern half of the region, deeper moisture remained entrenched over the southern half. With better moisture and instability in this area, scattered to widespread showers and thunderstorms formed over the higher terrain and drifted out over adjacent valleys. One of these storms encountered a source of modest surface vorticity, and began to weakly rotate, eventually producing a landspout tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One of several thunderstorms across Southeast Utah on the afternoon of July 20th began to rotate and produced a landspout tornado near Verdure in San Juan County, UT, along US Highway 191. This landspout was reported to the Grand Junction NWS office by a trained spotter, and this report was later confirmed by images and video provided by a media partner in the Salt Lake City NWS office's area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture across eastern Utah and western Colorado, in association with a weak monsoonal push, brought an increase in shower and thunderstorm coverage. This was the first day of the moisture surge, meaning that surface levels of the atmosphere remained unsaturated, favoring virga and strong, gusty outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms initially formed over the adjacent higher terrain of the eastern Uinta Mountains, before drifting over the Uinta Basin. As these storms moved over the town of Vernal, they produced wind gusts of 45-60 mph. The peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at the KVEL ASOS at Vernal Airport. No reports of damage or injury were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, with a strong 140 knot jet aloft wrapping around the trough's base. This jet, combined with a very tight southwesterly pressure gradient aloft, brought very strong winds to the region ahead of an impressive cold front. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph across valley locations, while mountain locations saw gusts up to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph, with the peak gust of 65 mph occurring at the Hole N The Rock mesonet station just off of US Highway 191, about 15 miles south of Moab, UT. Three other sites also recorded wind gusts above 58 mph during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, with a strong 140 knot jet aloft wrapping around the trough's base. This jet, combined with a very tight southwesterly pressure gradient aloft, brought very strong winds to the region ahead of an impressive cold front. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph across valley locations, while mountain locations saw gusts up to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 59 mph, with the peak gust of 59 mph occurring at the KCNY ASOS located at Canyonlands Airport. One other site also recorded a gust above 58 mph during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, with a strong 140 knot jet aloft wrapping around the trough's base. This jet, combined with a very tight southwesterly pressure gradient aloft, brought very strong winds to the region ahead of an impressive cold front. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph across valley locations, while mountain locations saw gusts up to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone  ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph, with the peak gust of 66 mph occurring at the Vernal CWOP station in Vernal, UT. Three additional sites recorded gusts above 58 mph during this event. The KVEL ASOS, located at the Vernal Airport, recorded a peak gust of 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, with a strong 140 knot jet aloft wrapping around the trough's base. This jet, combined with a very tight southwesterly pressure gradient aloft, brought very strong winds to the region ahead of an impressive cold front. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 65 mph across valley locations, while mountain locations saw gusts up to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to nearly 60 mph, with the peak gust of 58 mph recorded at the Monument Valley Utah Department of Transportation weather station. This was the only location to record a gust at or above 58 mph. The KBDG AWOS in Blanding, UT recorded a gust to 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As southwesterly wind strengthened across the Four Corners Region ahead of a potent cold front and unseasonably strong Pacific trough, these winds picked up dust from the deserts of northern Arizona. This dust, along with dust pick up across the lower elevations of southeast Utah, were transported as far north as the I-70 corridor in far western Colorado and eastern Utah, including the Grand Valley and areas around Moab.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Combinations of social media reports and web cam images from across the zone indicated dense areas of blowing dust, with visibility estimated to have been reduced to less than 1/2 mile. The worst of this was reported in and around the Monument Valley area of southeast Utah, where estimated winds of 45-55 mph lofted dust in from northern Arizona. Dust continued tracking north through the zone, but visibility improved above 1 mile based on various observation sources.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initially modest cool season system and associated cold front brought precipitation to areas of Utah. For this period of the event, Utah's central mountains received slightly more notable snow, with limited impacts elsewhere through the state. This system then evolved into a cutoff low, which more or less meandered around the California/Arizona/Utah border region before eventually becoming reabsorbed into the greater flow several days later. Due to this, several prone locations in northern Utah valleys saw somewhat persistent strong easterly downslope winds, while areas in southern Utah (both low elevations and mountains) saw periods of impactful snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Seeley Creek, Farnsworth Lake, and Pine Creek recorded 0.9 to 1.6 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 21 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system approached the region for the start of March, with very anomalously strong low level flow noted ahead of a cold frontal boundary. This strong low level flow resulted in a prolonged period of strong sustained and gusty winds across almost the entirety of Utah. As the cold front moved into northern Utah, some areas experienced  convective precipitation and associated strong wind gusts. Winds began to subside following the passage of the cold front, but the colder air allowed for precipitation to transition to snow, yielding accumulating snow across much of the area. Mountain locations which remained sufficiently cold throughout the event received fairly substantial snow accumulations in addition to the prolonged strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Parley's Summit, Lookout Peak, and Parrish Creek recorded roughly 1.2 to 1.8 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 18 to 27 inches of snow. Strong winds and wind gusts were also observed at various mesonet sites in the zone through the event. Some observations include a peak wind gust of 91 mph at the Logan Peak mesonet site 3/2 at 12:00 pm MST, a wind gust of 77 mph at the Sherwood Hills mesonet site 3/2 at 1:30 am MST, and 77 mph at the Crow Mountain mesonet site 3/2 at 12:10 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool season system impacted the area late in March and resulted in an unsettled period of weather and moderate snow accumulations for many high elevation locations around Utah. This system was characterized by an initial digging shortwave which then left the area in a broader unsettled longwave trough with embedded impulses, which triggered multiple rounds of precipitation over the course of several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Parley's Summit, Ben Lomond Peak, and Lookout Peak recorded 1.7 to 3.0 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 18 to 38 inches of snow. Additionally, a report was received from the snow safety community of 19 inches of snow at Snowbasin - Base.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Cascade Mountain, Mill-D North, and Atwater recorded 1.9 to 4.0 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 18 to 54 inches of snow. Additionally, some reports received from the snow safety community include 39 inches of snow at Alta - Collins, 37 inches of snow at Brighton - Crest, and 25 inches of snow at Park City Mountain. An additional report of 32.6 inches was also received from a COOP observer in Alta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another northern stream trough dug southward along the Pacific Coast before pivoting eastward into the Great Basin and Desert Southwest. Embedded energy in the preceding southwesterly flow and eventual frontal passage with the trough axis shifting eastward resulted in multiple rounds of precipitation, yielding accumulating mountain snow to close out the month of March. Additionally, an avalanche occurred and resulted in an injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Kimberly Mine, Castle Valley, and Kolob recorded 1.0 to 3.4 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 42 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of very strong winds were noted, with some mesonet sites observing both sustained winds in excess of 40 mph for an hour or more, and gusts in excess of 58 mph. The earliest observed high wind gust was noted at the CALLAO GATE mesonet site at 5:35 am MST (60 mph), and the final observed high wind gust was noted at the CAUSEWAY mesonet site at 1:45 pm MST (58 mph). A peak wind gust of 77 mph was observed at the CAMEL BACK MTN mesonet site at 12:05 pm MST. Some additional selected gusts at mesonet sites in the zone included 74 mph at Gunnison Island, 73 mph at I-80 @ mp 29, 71 mph at INTERSTATE 80, and 64 mph at I-80 at MP 1. At least some of the strongest of the gusts may have been partially influenced by convective segments along the advancing cold front. Due to the winds, I-80 was closed from the morning hours of 3/2 onward until winds began to subside behind the frontal passage later in the afternoon. Aside from the winds, accumulating snow was observed at both mountain and lower elevations, but widespread warning amounts were not noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had moved in over eastern Utah and western Colorado by the night of April 6th to the 7th. This already cold air was compounded by clear and calm conditions that promoted radiational cooling across much of the region. At this point in the year, only the lowest elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado had acquired enough Growing Degree Days to warrant freeze highlights, as important agricultural assets, with particular emphasis on fruit trees, were beginning to bloom and a hard freeze event could be disastrous. Luckily, many of these crops were not damaged by this freeze event, even though many locations did hit hard freeze criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures across the zone ranged from 32F to 16F, with the minimum temperature of 16F occurring at the Kane Gulch RAWS, KAGU1. 9 of the 10 available sites registered temperatures at or below 32 degrees for at least 3 hours, and these temperatures lasted for, on average, 8 hours during the event, which qualifies this as a Freeze Event. In addition, 5 of the available 10 sites registered temperatures at or below 28 degrees for at least 3 hours, with the average duration being 4.5 hours. This qualifies this event as a Hard Freeze event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures across the zone ranged from 32F to 23F. The minimum temperature of the event was 23F, recorded at the UTHAD site Harley Dome, at MP 226 on I-70. In addition, the KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport recorded a minimum temperature of 25F. 8 of the available 16 sites recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with these temperatures lasting for on average 5.5 hours. Only 4 of the available 16 sites recorded temperatures at or below 28 degrees for at least 3 hours, with these sites seeing an average duration of 3.5 hours. Based on these data, this event in Utah Zone 027 qualifies only as a Freeze.

Additionally, 5 of the 7 available sites recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours, with these lasting for on average 3.5 hours. These data indicate this zone experienced both a Freeze and a Hard Freeze event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado briefly touched down near Herriman, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 40.53, -112.01. Location and time are estimated from reports and radar. A brief landspout was reported via numerous photos and videos. One photo in particular appears to confirm the circulation briefly touched the ground by kicking up dust in a construction area. An NWS employee surveyed the approximate location and talked to several witnesses in the area, but could not locate any damage, and no damage was reported to the local police departments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very deep tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alberto combined with monsoonal moisture was forced through eastern Utah and western Colorado due to a large high pressure ridge over the Central U.S. and a deep low pressure trough over the Great Basin. This deep moisture combined with strong instability and shear to produce widespread severe weather across eastern Utah and western Colorado with thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding and debris flows. One particular storm even produced a landspout tornado in southeast Utah. Frequent lightning also resulted in lightning damage in Montrose, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer near La Sal, UT reported 1 inch hail in association with a severe thunderstorm that passed over the area on the morning of the 21st.

One particular storm even produced a landspout tornado in southeast Utah. Frequent lightning also resulted in lightning damage in Montrose, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred due to heavy rains from a thunderstorm near Valley of the Gods and northeast of Mexican Hat. Fast moving waters covered Highway 163 near Valley of the Gods for a distance of 540 meters and 1 meter deep. Cottonwood Wash had major flood, surrounding house and filling flood plain. San Juan River spiked from 8.3 ft (2670 cfs) to 17.99 ft (20,700 cfs) near Mexican Hat. 28 people were trapped and stranded while on a river trip on the San Juan River where Lime Creek enters and eventually rescued. However, their boats and gear were swept away and lost in floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initially modest cool season system and associated cold front brought precipitation to areas of Utah. For this period of the event, Utah's central mountains received slightly more notable snow, with limited impacts elsewhere through the state. This system then evolved into a cutoff low, which more or less meandered around the California/Arizona/Utah border region before eventually becoming reabsorbed into the greater flow several days later. Due to this, several prone locations in northern Utah valleys saw somewhat persistent strong easterly downslope winds, while areas in southern Utah (both low elevations and mountains) saw periods of impactful snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US-89 at MP 406 Weber River mesonet site measured the first wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with an initial high wind gust of 62 mph observed 3/14 at 10:10 am MST. The US-89 at Park Lane mesonet site measured the last wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with a final high wind gust of 58 mph observed 3/17 at 8:40 am MST. The US-89 at Park Lane mesonet site also measured the zone's peak wind gust during the event, with a gust of 72 mph observed 3/16 at 6:55 am MST. The Ogden-Hinckley Airport ASOS also measured several high wind gusts in excess of 58 mph. Finally, some additional selected observed gusts at mesonet sites in the zone through the event time included 66 mph at EW1185 Kaysville, 60 mph at Layton, 58  mph at I-15 At MP 333 Antelope Drive, and 58 mph at I-15 Farr West. Many sites recorded multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph throughout the event.

Further derived analyses suggests similar snow totals were likely realized across other more observational sparse areas of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep subtropical moisture increased across eastern Utah and western Colorado and combined with forcing from a strong low pressure trough digging across the Intermountain West to result in widespread severe thunderstorms. These storms produced severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain that resulted in flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong to severe thunderstorm tracked from west to east along I-70 on the afternoon of the 27th, producing strong to severe wind gusts in addition to other hazards. Wind gusts ranged from 45 mph to 61 mph. The peak gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Utah Department of Transportation mesonet site I-70 at MP 226 Harley Dome. This was the only location to see a severe wind gust with this storm, although this same storm did go on to produce more severe wind gusts at locations just to the east, in Mesa County, CO. No reports of wind damage were received with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a closed low over the Pacific Northwest deepened in the hours leading up to this event, the pressure gradient around a quasi-stationary cold front over northeastern Utah began to strengthen. This led to strengthening southwest winds out ahead of the front over much of northeastern Utah and northwestern Colorado during the day on the 17th. The strongest winds were expected over the eastern Uinta Mountains in northeast Colorado, decreasing as one moved south and east away from the stationary front and the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts across the eastern Uinta Mountains ranged from 45 mph to the peak gust of 75 mph during the day on the 17th. The peak gust was recorded at the Utah Department of Highways mesonet site Sr-43 at Mp 0.5 West Stateline, close to the Wyoming border, west of the town of Manila.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph was measured at the Canyonlands Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system approached the region for the start of March, with very anomalously strong low level flow noted ahead of a cold frontal boundary. This strong low level flow resulted in a prolonged period of strong sustained and gusty winds across almost the entirety of Utah. As the cold front moved into northern Utah, some areas experienced  convective precipitation and associated strong wind gusts. Winds began to subside following the passage of the cold front, but the colder air allowed for precipitation to transition to snow, yielding accumulating snow across much of the area. Mountain locations which remained sufficiently cold throughout the event received fairly substantial snow accumulations in addition to the prolonged strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Murray's Hill mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 70 mph as a result of a convective section of a cold frontal boundary advancing through the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved over the Moab area on the afternoon of the 27th, putting down very heavy rain and high rain rates. This caused flooding and debris flows across the area, including near Arches National Park. Flooding and debris flows outside the park on Highway 191 led to the closure of the road. Additionally, there were reports and photos of flooding and rockfalls/debris flows within the park on the scenic drive. These flows made the only entrance/exit road from the park impassable for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system approached the region for the start of March, with very anomalously strong low level flow noted ahead of a cold frontal boundary. This strong low level flow resulted in a prolonged period of strong sustained and gusty winds across almost the entirety of Utah. As the cold front moved into northern Utah, some areas experienced  convective precipitation and associated strong wind gusts. Winds began to subside following the passage of the cold front, but the colder air allowed for precipitation to transition to snow, yielding accumulating snow across much of the area. Mountain locations which remained sufficiently cold throughout the event received fairly substantial snow accumulations in addition to the prolonged strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tintic mesonet site measured the first wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with an initial high wind gust of 59 mph observed 3/2 at 7:10 am MST. The EW4925 Provo mesonet site (personal weather station) measured the last wind gust exceeding high wind criteria, with a final high wind gust of 62 mph observed 3/2 at 4:25 pm MST. The Tintic mesonet site measured the zone's peak wind gust during the event, with a gust of 82 mph observed 3/2 at 2:00 pm MST. Finally, some additional selected observed gusts at mesonet sites in the zone through the event time included 68 mph at EMC-PonyEx, 66 mph at SR-68 at MP 15 Mosida, and 60 mph at Flight Park South. Many sites recorded multiple wind gusts in excess of 58 mph throughout the event. Areas of heavy snow and accumulations were also noted through the zone. Some storm total snow accumulation reports include 12 inches at Woodland Hills, 8 inches at Payson, and 6 inches at Spanish Fork Power House.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Prairie Fire started in mid September 19 miles west-northwest of Mack, Colorado just north of Interstate 70 on the Utah side near the Colorado-Utah stateline due to a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Prairie Fire started in mid September 19 miles west-northwest of Mack, Colorado just north of Interstate 70 on the Utah side near the Colorado-Utah stateline due to a lightning strike. The fire grew to 1,921 acres before it was contained a couple days later in mid September. The estimated cost to date was $375,000.
