EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another northern stream trough dug southward along the Pacific Coast before pivoting eastward into the Great Basin and Desert Southwest. Embedded energy in the preceding southwesterly flow and eventual frontal passage with the trough axis shifting eastward resulted in multiple rounds of precipitation, yielding accumulating mountain snow to close out the month of March. Additionally, an avalanche occurred and resulted in an injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Smith and Morehouse, Chepeta, and Brown Duck recorded 1.0 to 2.0 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 25 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another northern stream trough dug southward along the Pacific Coast before pivoting eastward into the Great Basin and Desert Southwest. Embedded energy in the preceding southwesterly flow and eventual frontal passage with the trough axis shifting eastward resulted in multiple rounds of precipitation, yielding accumulating mountain snow to close out the month of March. Additionally, an avalanche occurred and resulted in an injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three skiers skiing on the east face of Mt. Raymond in Millcreek Canyon triggered an avalanche, with a width of approximately 200 feet, a depth of 2 feet, and a vertical run of approximately 800 feet. One of the skiers was caught in the slide and forced into trees at a high rate of speed, which resulted in the skier breaking an arm. The other skiers were able to sling the injured skiers arm, and the injured skier was then able to  ski out and seek treatment at a medical facility. No specific time was detailed in the avalanche report, so time of avalanche is approximated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very deep tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Alberto combined with monsoonal moisture was forced through eastern Utah and western Colorado due to a large high pressure ridge over the Central U.S. and a deep low pressure trough over the Great Basin. This deep moisture combined with strong instability and shear to produce widespread severe weather across eastern Utah and western Colorado with thunderstorms producing severe wind gusts, large hail and heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding and debris flows. One particular storm even produced a landspout tornado in southeast Utah. Frequent lightning also resulted in lightning damage in Montrose, Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough dropped into the Great Basin, bringing well above normal moisture to eastern Utah and western Colorado in the realm of 200 to 300 percent above normal. Cold air associated with this storm system in addition to the moisture and strong winds from the upper level jet, led to widespread moderate to heavy snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The heaviest snowfall occurred over the mountains of southwest and west-central Colorado where 1 to 2 feet of snow fell. Winds gusted upwards of 45 to 65 mph on average across most mountain ranges, with peak wind gusts of 80 to 90 mph over the San Juan mountains leading to blowing snow and whiteout conditions. Snow levels remained high with lower valleys receiving primarily rain with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted 40 to 55 mph at some Highway 191 and Highway 43 sites.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 38-year-old man drowned Saturday in the waters of the northwest portion of Willard Bay during stormy conditions. This individual attempted to rescue another who went into the water to rescue another who had a life jacket. Nearby wind gusts with an associated outflow boundary ranged from 28 mph (Willard POE RWIS, located just east of Willard Bay) to 32 mph (Great Salt Lake Minerals Site, located just SW of Willard Bay). Small showers with microbursts were also observed in the area, which may have also impacted Willard Bay, in addition to the more consolidated outflow boundary that was moving through the Northern Wasatch Front from south to north during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system dropped southward across eastern Utah from the Intermountain West, bringing significant snowfall to the eastern Uinta mountains before the system stalled near the Four Corners over northern Arizona. This allowed significant moisture to keep heavy snowfall ongoing over the San Juan Mountains for a few days before the low pressure system lifted northeast across west-central and northwest Colorado, bringing some additional snowfall to the northern and central mountains. The San Juan Mountains received the most snowfall due to the nearly stationary nature of this system with amounts upwards of 2 to 3 feet. Winds were also particularly strong over the southern mountains due to the presence of a strong upper level jet. This was the first significant winter storm of the season after a period of above normal warmth for early to mid October. Milder temperatures though kept snow levels above 8000 feet with most lower valleys seeing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 13 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds stayed below 45 mph.

The fire grew to 157 acres before it was contained in late October. The estimated final cost was $3 million. The homes were not impacted as fire crews were able to contain and suppress the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold winter system began impacting the area on October 28th, bringing mountain snow and valley rain to eastern Utah and western Colorado. The cold air behind this system led to an overall cold night across eastern Utah and western Colorado, with portions of Southeast Utah and Southwest Colorado seeing lows drop below freezing under clearing skies and strong radiational cooling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five of the nine reporting stations available for verification recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, though none recorded temperatures at or below 28F. This means at least 50% of the zone experienced a freeze, but not a season-ending one. The minimum recorded temperature was 29F, while the minimum recorded at the Kane Gulch RAWS was 31F. Minimum temperatures ranged from 29F to 32F. Temperatures were at or below 32F for on average 3.5 hours, with a maximum of 10 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system approaching eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west drove very strong southwesterly winds out ahead of it. These winds gusted 80-85 mph over the higher terrain, and 60-65 mph across some of the valley locations. As the system moved overhead and precipitation began, winds diminished on the night of October 29th into the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty southwesterly winds peaked over Grand County, Utah in the late morning and early afternoon of October 29th. The highest recorded gust was 61 mph recorded at the KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, about 10 miles north of Moab. This was recorded between 10AM and 11AM MDT. Gusts around the zone ranged from 45 mph to the peak of 61 mph. No reports of damage were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and cold air advection in the wake a strong late fall storm brough sub-freezing temperatures to many lower valley locations on the night of October 30th into the 31st. This event brought a season ending freeze to several locations, while a few others squeaked through with just a general freeze event. The central valleys of eastern Utah and western Colorado were most affecting, being some of the last locations still to not have had a season-ending freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of the twenty reporting sites available for verification recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, though only four of the twenty recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours. This means that at least 50% of the zone experienced a freeze, but it was overall not season-ending. The minimum temperature recorded was 27F, while the minimum recorded at the KVEL ASOS in Vernal was 28F. Minimums ranged from 27F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted for on average 8.5 hours, with a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 5 hours. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted for on average 1 hour, with a maximum of 7 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the days prior to August 17th, a dome of high pressure had been in control of eastern Utah and western Colorado, with a plume of monsoonal moisture well to the west in the Central Great Basin. As a strong Pacific trough moved in the Pacific Northwest, the entire system of high pressure and monsoonal plume were nudged eastward, pushing the plume into eastern Utah. Surface layers remained dry, as it often takes several days of monsoonal moisture to saturate the atmosphere. This setup favors strong to severe winds with any shower or thunderstorm that forms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that formed over the higher terrain south and west of Canyonlands Airport in eastern Utah eventually drifted over the adjacent lower terrain. As it did so, it produced two wind gusts to 58 mph, in addition to wind gusts of lower values. These gusts were recorded at KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, 16 miles northwest of Moab. No reports of damages or injuries were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With deep monsoonal moisture in place, as well as around 1000 J/kg of instability and some bulk shear aloft, widespread strong to severe thunderstorms formed across the Four Corners region. This resulted in multiple severe threats in eastern Utah which included large hail and flash flooding due to heavy rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain due to thunderstorms over the area resulted in 1.5 to 2 inch per hour rainfall rates with pea size hail mixed in. This resulted in flooding across downtown Moab with 2 feet of water flowing through downtown. Mill Creek saw a rise in water levels which runs through town with water cresting the roadway bridge. Water was also seen running over some driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With deep monsoonal moisture in place, as well as around 1000 J/kg of instability and some bulk shear aloft, widespread strong to severe thunderstorms formed across the Four Corners region. This resulted in multiple severe threats in eastern Utah which included large hail and flash flooding due to heavy rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong to severe storm passed over Thompson Springs and produced hail ranging from 0.7 to 1.25 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With deep monsoonal moisture in place, as well as around 1000 J/kg of instability and some bulk shear aloft, widespread strong to severe thunderstorms formed across the Four Corners region. This resulted in multiple severe threats in eastern Utah which included large hail and flash flooding due to heavy rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm passed over the Fry Canyon area and produced hail up to nickel size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier air working in behind a shortwave trough across the north resulted in more surface heating to act on sufficient instability and remnant moisture to produce stronger storms. Some shear was also present across the north leading to some stronger storms that produced up to nickel size hail across Uintah County in northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm passed over Naples area in Uintah County and produced hail up to nickel size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As several weak systems moved through the area during the last week of 2024, they helped to create pools of rich moisture trapped in the lower elevation valleys. These pools of moisture condensed during the overnight period into widespread areas of dense fog. The lower elevation river valleys of eastern Utah and western Colorado were mainly effected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 2:30AM local time, the KCNY ASOS at Canyonlands Airport, about 10 miles north of Moab, UT, began reporting visibility reduced to 1/4 mile or less. In addition, satellite imagery indicated that the dense fog was widespread throughout the zone, particularly along well travelled highway corridors. As the sun rose, road cameras provided by the Utah Department of Transportation allowed for visual confirmation of the extent of the fog. This fog lingered through the morning before beginning to finally lift around 12PM local time. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While drier air moving in to the region began to scour out the rich pockets of low level moisture across the lower elevation valleys of eastern Utah and western Colorado, deep moisture lingered in the eastern Uinta Basin. This moisture condensed during the overnight hours, creating dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 6:45AM local time, the KVEL ASOS at Vernal Airport, in Vernal, UT began reporting visibilities of 1/4 mile or less. Additionally, satellite imagery helped to confirm that the dense fog was more than just localized at the airport. As the sun rose, road cameras provided by the Utah Department of Transportation added visual conformation of the extent of the dense fog, particularly along the US Highway 40 corridor. This fog lingered into the morning before beginning to lift after 10AM local time, as dry air and sunshine began to erode the surface moisture. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled pattern that characterized the last week of the month of December brought widespread precipitation to eastern Utah and western Colorado with regularity. This precipitation created pools of rich low level moisture trapped in the lower elevation valleys across the region. As skies cleared and temperatures cooled each night, dense fog then developed in each of these valleys, often widespread. Thanks to the low sun angle, this fog would linger well into the daylight hours before finally lifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 9:45AM local time, the KVEL ASOS began reporting visibilities of less than 1/2 mile. Satellite imagery, and road cameras provided by the Utah Department of Transportation confirmed the widespread nature of the dense fog in and around the more populated parts of the eastern Uinta Basin. The fog began to lift around 12PM local time. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

These pools of moisture condensed during the overnight period into widespread areas of dense fog. The lower elevation river valleys of eastern Utah and western Colorado were mainly effected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 2:30Am local time, the KVEL ASOS at Vernal Airport in Vernal, UT began reporting visibilities of 1/4 mile or less. In addition, satellite imagery indicated that this dense fog was widespread. As the sun rose, road cameras provided by the Utah Department of Transportation allowed for visual confirmation of the extent of this fog. Low sun angles allowed the fog to last through the morning and into the early afternoon before finally beginning to lift. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowboarders died in avalanches in Millcreek Canyon and Big Cottonwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was snowboarding in the Porter Fork area of Millcreek Canyon when he was caught by an avalanche. He was dug from the snow several days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowboarders died in avalanches in Millcreek Canyon and Big Cottonwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder died in an avalanche in Silver Fork of Big Cottonwood Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought strong winds prior to rain and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at the Vernon Hill mesonet site. Other wind gusts include 71 mph at the SR-201 at I-80 mesonet site and 68 mph at the I-80 at MP 79 Timpie mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought strong winds prior to rain and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at the DW6120 Sandy mesonet site. Other wind gusts include 58 mph at the FW1924 West Jordan mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storm systems brought mountain snow throughout Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 35.5 inches with 4.91 inches of snow water equivalent was measured at Alta. Other snowfall accumulations include 34.0 inches with 3.79 inches of snow water equivalent at Brighton, 27.0 inches with 3.08 inches of snow water equivalent at Snowbird, and 20.0 inches with 2.42 inches of snow water equivalent at Solitude.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storm systems brought mountain snow throughout Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 18.0 inches with 1.76 inches of snow water equivalent was measured near Heber City. Snow water equivalent at several SNOTEL sites ranged from 1.2-2.1 inches, with an estimated 12-21 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storm systems brought mountain snow throughout Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalent at several SNOTEL sites ranged from 1.3-2.5 inches, with an estimated 13-25 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storm systems brought mountain snow throughout Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of 29.0 inches with 3.51 inches of snow water equivalent was measured at Snowbasin. Other snowfall accumulations include 23.0 inches with 3.20 inches of snow water equivalent at Powder Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level shortwave trough moved across the northern half of the area, producing light to moderate snowfall accumulations to the Eastern Uinta Mountains, Park Mountains, Gore Mountains and Elk Mountains. The remaining mountain ranges received far less snowfall with accumulations in the lower elevations limited due to mild air ahead of this system. The Yampa River Basin, however, received some light to moderate snowfall accumulations as the cold front dropped southward over northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 13 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong Atmospheric River event brought prolonged period of heavy snowfall accumulations to southeast Utah and much of western Colorado in the days leading up to Thanksgiving, resulting in upwards of 2 to 3 feet of snow for many mountain areas with locally higher amounts above 40 inches. Some higher valley locations, in particular the Gunnison Basin and Upper Yampa River Basin, received over a foot of snow. Some areas along Interstate 70 also received moderate to heavy snowfall, including valley locations. Travel was significantly impacted during this event in the days leading up to Thanksgiving with road closures. Some lower valleys received over an inch of rain as well with light snow accumulations once the rain changed over to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with 7.2 at East Willow Creek.

Some lower valleys received over an inch of rain as well with light snow accumulations once the rain changed over to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 25 to 35 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 13 to 15 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains. 5 inches of snow was reported at Monticello 0.4 SW.  Winds generally gusted in the 15 to 35 mph range with a peak gust of 44 mph at La Sal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly mild decaying atmospheric river brushed through Utah in late November, resulting in accumulating mountain snow. This system also brought a small amount of accumulating snow to higher elevation bench locations near terrain, but most lower elevations saw precipitation fall primarily in the form of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 10:02 pm, a Toyota Prius travelling southbound on I-15 lost control and hit a concrete barrier near milepost 354, eventually coming to rest facing sideways on the interstates shoulder. A male passenger exited the car, while the female passenger remained and opened her door. A second vehicle, a Ford Expedition, hit the male outside of the car and then crashed into the passenger side of the Prius head-on, killing the female passenger. The male passenger outside of the car was transported to the hospital in critical condition. The 2 left lanes of southbound I-15 were closed for an undisclosed amount of time for accident investigation, with Utah Highway Patrol ultimately believing weather to be the primary factor. During the time around the accident, lake enhanced snow showers were moving through the area, with a nearby DOT sensor reporting moderate to heavy snowfall, slushy road conditions, pavement temperatures dropping below freezing, and snowfall rates of a half inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough digging into the Great Basin gradually shifted through the region, resulting in multiple days of scattered thunderstorm development and associated severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake City International Airport ASOS station recorded wind gusts of 59 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly weak shortwave slipped by the forecast region to the north, but provided sufficient  synoptic support to yield scattered high-based convective development, and some convection resulted in severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mary's Lift mesonet station recorded a wind gust of 77 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and associated cold front moved through the area from the Pacific Northwest, bringing light to moderate snowfall accumulations to most mountain areas of western Colorado as well as southeast Utah with roughly 4 to 10 inches. Heavy snow bands ahead of and along the cold front contributed to some locally higher amounts in some areas. The low pressure system then tracked over the southern half of the area, resulting in heavier snowfall accumulations over the San Juan Mountains upwards of 1 to 2 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 9 to 13 inches of snow fell over the La Sal Mountains with 4 to 9 inches of snow over the Abajo Mountains. Winds generally gusted in the 30 to 40 mph range with a peak gust to 45 mph over La Sal on the morning of November 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed in advance of an approaching shortwave trough digging through the Pacific Northwest. This convection resulted in some isolated instances of severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WHITE SAGE mesonet station recorded an initial peak wind gust of 71 mph, followed by an additional gust of 63 mph, as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough dove southward and passed through Arizona and New Mexico, keeping much of eastern Utah and western Colorado in a deformation zone and allowing for widespread light to moderate accumulations of snow in the mountains. Mild temperatures and dry low levels ahead of this system limited snow accumulations in the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 inches of snow fell at East Willow Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly mild decaying atmospheric river brushed through Utah in late November, resulting in accumulating mountain snow. This system also brought a small amount of accumulating snow to higher elevation bench locations near terrain, but most lower elevations saw precipitation fall primarily in the form of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations including Jones Corral, Midway Valley, and Big Flat recorded from 1.1 to 4.1 inches of snow water equivalent, resulting in approximately 12 to 44 inches of snow.
